text:
Just saw it. My rating -- 7 out of 10.   Timothée Chalamet is a credible stand-in for Dylan. He has the idiosyncratic mannerisms. He learned to play the guitar for the film, and sings as a reasonable facsimile.  Monica Barbaro really shines in terms of her Joan Baez impression (speaking and singing). 
My critique is that this story is told in a way that leaves some false impressions.  It shows Baez  standing off to the side, stony-faced and seemingly horrified when Dylan "goes electric" at Newport '65. Firstly, in reality she was not even present at the festival that night.  Secondly, she wasn't some judgmental folk purist!  She had been on tour with Dylan in the UK the previous April and she had heard him perform electronically.  She herself was a big fan of Motown.  From what I've read about that night at Newport, some people were booing, but many were cheering and even those who were booing, it was mostly about the distortion rendering the vocals inaudible, not the fact that Dylan was using electric guitars.

text:
Is this movie completely accurate?
No.
Is this movie perfect?
No.
Does it matter?
Hell no.

Yes, the movie condenses the timeline. 
Yes, the movie alters situations.
Yes, it's not possible to cover everything in a nearly 2 1/2 hour movie.
But knowing who's involved in this picture, including Dylan himself, I guessed I would enjoy it, and I did, very much.

Ignore the biopic haters, the "know it all" fans, and the critics. 

I imagine Dylan fans will enjoy this movie and the enigma, songs, and joy that is Bob Dylan. 

How does it feel?
It feels great.

text: I’ve seen the documentary “no direction home” long ago, that was enough for me. Seems like TC studied BD’s mannerisms well and had a lot of professional training/help. Any sort of “era” film will work as a sort of romanticized “escapist” film into that time period, as the past is always easy to romanticize. People romanticize the future too but it’s because styled notions of what we consider “the future” is really just visions of it from the 80s, so easily romanticized. When it comes to music, the most inspired works almost have little to do with the artists themselves, lyrically it transcends the person as artist itself. So these sort of little life moments don’t really matter to me when listening to music. I don’t really need to know or see any of it. If you think the music itself has anything to do with these little romanticized moments in the past, that’s just ego. Same with Jeff Buckley when you listen to their lyrics. This is what it means when you serve the music. Those who compose in such a way that serves the music…it doesn’t matter much where they come from or how they’ve lived, if you’re really listening. The music itself is beyond them.

text: I wonder if the screenwriters bothered to read "A Freewheelin' Time" by Suze Rotolo, Dylan's serious New York girlfriend? This trailer looks like the "Sylvie Russo" character's personality, including calling Dylan out on some of his asshole behavior, will be attributed to Joan Baez rather than Suze. Suze, a red diaper baby, was an invaluable help to Dylan in writing his protest songs, getting the politics straight. She was also the first to discover his real identity, Robert Allen Zimmerman, middle class boy from Hibbing, Minnesota. Suze provided Dylan with an early, critical input on his songwriting. She loved him when he was "nobody" and wasn't blinded by his "genius." Dylan wrote some brilliant music, but not everything he wrote was tinged with "genius," as Baez still stubbornly insists it was, to this day. But, Joan Baez is still alive to influence and make demands about how she is depicted. Suze isn't. Baez was also the "It" girl of the moment back in 1963, powerful and influential back in the day, and Suze wasn't. I will probably see this. Chalamet looks and sounds good in the part. However, I am deeply skeptical about how Dylan's early rise in New York City will be portrayed. I know they will create composite characters, to fit the time frame of a 2-hour movie. I can't get used to movies being made about the cultural icons of my youth.

text: They’re making out for Dave Van Ronk to be the big anti-electric villain at Newport. This is just dumb. He was Dylan’s friend, Dylan slept on his couch the first week in the Village, and he wouldn’t have cared. He actually taught Bob his signature flat picking during that crucial first week. Who did care was Pete Seeger, who actually went looking for a fire axe, so he could cut the entire festival’s power cable. Of the performers, it was primarily the women who complained😢, like Maria Muldaur and Mimi Farina. The male performers mostly just envied Bob’s big electric balls, and wished 😊they had some.

text:
This film looks great and I love Dylan.. though the scene at 0:24 where Dylan is singing "Girl from the North Country", somebody says, “Who wrote this?”, and a guy replies that, “He wrote it” is a tad incorrect.
A lot of folk music has its roots in the "old country", Britain and Europe, obviously - Dylan travelled to England in the early 60’s, almost on a pilgrimage, and hung with lots of very good folk musicians there, and he essentially stole the arrangement of the traditional English song "Scarborough Fair" from Martin Carthy.  
Dylan changed the song a lot, but lyrically and melodically a lot of it is lifted from Carthy's version. Still, Dylan often spoke of that trip to England and credited English musicians like Carthy for some of his early inspiration... Just a shame the film didn't get the studio guys to say that about *another* Dylan song instead of "Girl from the North Country"

text: This Bob Dylan biopic is excellent. I hope James Mangold will be forgiven for Indiana Jones 5 with this one. Timothée Chalamet plays again a big role and will get a nomination or even his first win at the Academy Awards next year. Edward Norton and Elle Fanning are also very good and Boyd Holbrook as Johnny Cash is special (if Joaquin Phoenix had played the role again in this one it will have a bit more special) but I trust Boyd. However the one in the cast that I’m most excited about is Monica Barbaro. While have starred in a lot of TV-shows, she is maybe only known for playing Phoenix in Top Gun: Maverick (2022) and starring alongside Arnold Schwarzenegger in FUBAR (2023-). This is her second big movie so I hope she gets attention for future roles in movies and shows.

text:
I know this reads as cynical, but some things are truly best left alone. We have docs from D.A. Pennebaker and Scorsese that chronicle everything you'd want to see/know about Bob in this period, but we somehow need a poor imitation or re-telling of those events that will always be  less interesting than the actual footage/stories themselves. I can't get past Chalamet doing a Bob impression - feels a bit hollow for me.

Doing something interesting/inventive about Bob in a narrative feature film has already been done - Todd Hayne's "I'm Not There". Christian Bale, Heath Ledger, Cate Blanchett, and more already explored Dylan in a compelling way that didn't feel like a cheap, Oscar bait-y imitation. I may see this, but I'm skeptical that it'll be as interesting or emotionally impactful as it wants to be.
